items.title: Russia, Ukraine agree to 3-day ceasefire and prisoner swap
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump said Friday that the leaders of Russia and Ukraine have agreed to his request for a three-day ceasefire and an exchange of prisoners, adding that such a halt to hostilities could be the "beginning of the end" of the long war between them.

items.title: Air India cuts flights to Canada as jet fuel prices soar
items.summary_auto: Air India has cut flight volumes for April and May, with plans for more schedule trims in June and July, according to an internal memo to staff. Its round-trip flights scheduled between Toronto and Delhi fell to 31 in May versus 48 in March, a 35 per cent decrease, according to aviation data tracker Cirium.

items.title: Ottawa proposes suite of measures to streamline project approvals, complete review process within 1 year
items.summary_auto: The federal government is proposing legislative changes to streamline the review process for major projects, including energy projects such as pipelines. The changes are aimed at completing these reviews within a year.

items.title: Israeli strikes kill 5 in southern Lebanon as Hezbollah rockets hit open areas in Israel
items.summary_auto: Israeli airstrikes on southern Lebanon killed at least five people Friday, while the Iran-backed Hezbollah militant group fired rockets on northern Israel without inflicting any casualties.

items.title: Winnipeg-based retailer to liquidate all Warehouse One, Bootlegger stores
items.summary_auto: Winnipeg-based clothing retailer Warehouse One is moving to liquidate all of its 128 stores, including Bootlegger.

items.title: 3 people standing in front of North York synagogue shot at with replica firearm: police
items.summary_auto: Three people standing in front of a synagogue in North York Thursday night were shot at with a replica firearm in an incident that police say is being investigated as a hate-motivated offence.

items.title: Danielle Smith says she hopes MOU can be finalized in 'next number of days'
items.summary_auto: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said Friday she hopes the memorandum of understanding between her province and the federal government can be completed soon because Albertans need to see that "Canada can work."

items.title: After more than a year of litigation, former AHS CEO’s wrongful dismissal lawsuit has much to sort out
items.summary_auto: Civil cases, especially complex ones, can take years to weave through the legal system. But 14 months after Athana Mentzelopoulos launched her wrongful dismissal suit, many Albertans may wonder about the status of the high-profile case.

items.title: Hantavirus isn't COVID-19. Here's why contact tracing after the MV Hondius outbreak is different
items.summary_auto: Human-to-human transmission of the rodent-borne hantavirus may be quite rare, but a deadly outbreak that happened on board a cruise ship in the Atlantic Ocean now has health officials around the world — including here in Canada — monitoring for any potential spread.

items.title: Seed imports, licence buyback program part of federal support for P.E.I.'s oyster industry
items.summary_auto: In a news release Friday, the federal government has announced a series of support measures for Prince Edward Island’s oyster industry.

items.title: 2 youths among 8 charged in connection with smash-and-grab robberies in Ontario, police say
items.summary_auto: The Halton Regional Police Service has laid over 50 charges following several smash-and-grab robberies in Ontario between Jan. 25 and March 17, in what the deputy police chief is calling "the first major crackdown" on such thefts impacting businesses.

items.title: Canadian public health officials speaking now about hantavirus
Nintendo hiking price for Switch 2 consoles
items.summary_auto: The Japanese video-game company behind the Super Mario and Pokemon franchises announced it's raising prices on the Nintendo Switch 2, citing challenging business conditions.

items.title: Real Madrid fines Federico  Valverde, Aurelien Tchouameni more than $1.1M US for altercation
items.summary_auto: Real Madrid fined Federico Valverde and Aurelien Tchouameni half a million euros (the equivalent of $588,000 US) each on Friday for their altercation during practice.

items.title: Flare-ups in fighting continue as U.S. expects response from Iran on proposal to end hostilities
items.summary_auto: The United States said it expected a response from Iran to its latest proposal to end their war, even as American and Iranian ‌forces clashed again on Friday.

items.title: Canada's economy dropped 17,700 jobs in April as unemployment rose to six-month high
Canada's economy dropped 18,000 jobs in April as unemployment rose to 6-month high
items.summary_auto: April's loss means Canada has shed jobs in three of the fourth months so far this year, making for 112,000 jobs lost since January. The Statistics Canada data released on Friday indicates a continued weakness in the labour market, which has struggled in the ​face of U.S tariffs and trade uncertainty.

items.title: U of T, OCAD amongst Ontario universities impacted by Canvas cyber breach
Montreal ERs are getting some relief during Habs games in Stanley Cup playoffs
items.summary_auto: Hospital officials in the Montreal area say their emergency rooms generally saw dips in visits during playoff game nights.

items.title: Referendum bid by Alberta separatists stuck in legal limbo, even if petition has enough names
items.summary_auto: A legal case brought by First Nations over treaty rights prompted a temporary injunction in April. What happens next?

items.title: Caregivers in Canada are 'caught in a vicious cycle,' new report finds
items.summary_auto: A new report from the Canadian Centre for Caregiving Excellence has found that caregivers are caught in a bind, having to work more to meet the financial demands of care — and simultaneously work less to meet the time demands of care.

items.title: B.C. medical regulator heads to court to stop 'Cancer Guy' from dispensing medical advice
items.summary_auto: The College of Physicians and Surgeons of B.C. is seeking a permanent injunction to prevent the founder of a popular online "personalized cancer care and precision oncology service" from assessing patients and dispensing medical advice.

items.title: Manitoba's only potash mine nears commercial production after nearly 20 years of planning
items.summary_auto: The only potash mine in Manitoba plans to begin commercial production next month after nearly two decades of development.

items.title: This ski hill wants to save its snow for next season. Can it be done?
items.summary_auto: For snow lovers in the Maritimes, this past winter was one of the best in years. Now, Nova Scotia's Ski Martock wants to save that snow for the slopes next season. CBC meteorologist Ryan Snoddon explains.

items.title: Spain prepares for evacuations as hantavirus-hit cruise ship heads for Canary Islands
items.summary_auto: Spanish authorities on Friday were preparing to receive more than 140 passengers and crew members on board a hantavirus-stricken cruise ship headed for the Canary Islands, where health officials have said they will perform careful evacuations.

items.title: 'Will I have a job tomorrow?' Telus worker asks as AI tools spread
items.summary_auto: Some call centre agents at a major Canadian telecom company say they fear for their jobs as AI takes an increasing presence in their workplace. A Telus employee in B.C. says a company requirement to use an AI "co-pilot" on phone calls, part of a suite of AI tools Telus uses internally, has her concerned she's training a system that will eventually take her job.

items.title: Why do people still take cruises? Convenience, value outweigh health risks, experts say
items.summary_auto: By all accounts, the cruise industry is thriving, and tourism experts say they don't see that changing.

items.title: 'Mental well-being' biggest fear for thousands of seafarers stranded in the Strait of Hormuz
items.summary_auto: Food and water remain a significant concern for many of the thousands of seafarers stranded on vessels in the Strait of Hormuz. But the "the biggest fear for everybody is their mental well-being," says the general secretary of the International Transport Workers' Federation.

items.title: As AI creeps into telecoms, call centre agents worry they'll be replaced
items.summary_auto: Some call centre agents at a major Canadian telecom company say they fear for their jobs as AI takes an increasing presence in their workplace. A Telus employee in B.C. says a company requirement to use an AI "co-pilot" on phone calls, part of a suite of AI tools Telus uses internally, has her concerned she's training a system that will eventually take her job.

items.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster vow to fight breakup after monopoly verdict
items.summary_auto: Live Nation sat down with the CBC’s Dave Seglins in the company’s first feature interview since a U.S. federal jury verdict found the entertainment giant abused its monopoly and overcharged fans.

items.title: This woman’s identity was stolen in a CRA hack. Why hasn’t the impostor been charged in her case?
items.summary_auto: A B.C. nurse whose identity was stolen after her Canada Revenue Agency account was hacked says the CRA seems uninterested in pursuing the impostor who stole the public’s money after receiving a bogus refund in her name.

items.title: Probe alleging 'El Chapo' sons fed victims to tigers led to case against top Mexican politician: court docs
items.summary_auto: While the Mexican government insists it needs more proof before acting on a U.S. request to hand over the former governor of Sinaloa, court records suggest the case against Rubén Rocha stems from a sprawling investigation into the sons of former Sinaloa cartel boss Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán Loera.

items.title: How the U.S. blockade is starting to hurt Iran's economy
items.summary_auto: Soon after he ordered a naval blockade in mid-April, U.S. President Donald Trump claimed Iran's pipelines would "explode" within a few days from the inability to export crude oil. While that extreme prediction didn't come true, there's evidence the blockade is starting to hurt Iran's economy.

items.title: A look back at David Attenborough's work as he turns 100
items.summary_auto: Britain's David Attenborough, who has for decades been the world's most authoritative voice on the natural world and whose documentaries have been watched by hundreds of millions, celebrates his 100th birthday today. Here's a look back at his work over the years.

items.title: Quebec ombudsman calls mistreatment of Innu inmate who died a ‘violation of human dignity’
items.summary_auto: Philippe Pinette, a 40-year-old Innu man from Uashat, near Sept-Îles on Quebec's North Shore, died in hospital on Sept. 19, 2022, while under the care of the Quebec City detention centre. The province’s ombudsman pointed out multiple failures in the care provided.

items.title: Mortal Kombat II is brainless. That's why it works
items.summary_auto: Mortal Kombat is back for another round. But is what is clearly little more than a fan-service action-vehicle actually somehow good? A little bit. Maybe. Sort of?

items.title: Teen charged with murder of woman, 2 daughters in Brockville, Ont.
items.summary_auto: Police in Brockville, Ont., have charged a 17-year-old with first-degree murder after the bodies of a woman and her two young daughters were found inside a home in the eastern Ontario city on Thursday.

items.title: UBC, SFU among thousands of universities affected by Canvas software cyber breach
items.summary_auto: The University of British Columbia and Simon Fraser University, B.C. 's two biggest universities by student enrolment, say a cyber breach of the Canvas learning software could affect students' personal information.
